common_name: Heat Wave II Tomato (Gulf South High-Heat Fruit-Setting Hybrid Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Heat Wave II's primary advantage is fruit set at temperatures above 95°F — when most standard tomato varieties drop flowers without setting fruit, Heat Wave II continues to produce.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Heat Wave II is one of the most important tomato introductions for Gulf South gardeners — a hybrid specifically developed for heat tolerance to maintain fruit set during the high-temperature periods (daytime highs above 90–95°F) that cause standard tomato varieties to drop flowers without setting fruit. In zone 9, most standard tomato varieties produce well in spring (March–May) then cease fruiting when summer heat arrives in June; Heat Wave II's genetic ability to set fruit at high temperatures extends the productive season into summer conditions that defeat other varieties. The medium-large (6–8 oz), round, globe-shaped fruits have bright-red color, firm flesh, and good fresh-eating flavor — not the finest gourmet tomato, but extremely reliable production in conditions that eliminate the competition. Heat Wave II is a determinate (bush-type) tomato growing 4–5 feet that can be grown with minimal staking. Disease resistance includes V, F, FF, N, T — a comprehensive disease package for Gulf South conditions where soilborne disease pressure is high. For zone 9 gardeners who want tomatoes through the summer rather than only spring and fall, Heat Wave II is the primary recommendation.

common_name: Solar Fire Tomato (AAS-Winning Heat-Adapted Gulf South Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Solar Fire's All-America Selections recognition reflects outstanding performance across multiple growing regions, including the hot, humid Gulf South.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Solar Fire is an All-America Selections-recognized tomato hybrid developed at the University of Florida for superior performance in hot climates — combining good heat tolerance for extended fruit set with excellent fresh-eating flavor, disease resistance, and attractive fruit appearance. The medium-large (8–10 oz), round, firm, bright-red fruits are well-suited to Gulf South conditions — they hold well on the vine and in post-harvest storage better than some softer hybrid types. Solar Fire carries resistance to the major tomato diseases of the humid Gulf South including Verticillium wilt, Fusarium wilt (races 1 and 2), Alternaria stem canker, Gray leaf spot, and Nematodes — a disease package that significantly reduces the fungicide and nematicide inputs required for production. The determinate to semi-determinate growth habit reaches 4–6 feet with moderate staking requirements. Solar Fire typically performs well for zone 9 gardeners in both spring (February–June) and fall (August–November) planting windows. The spring crop produces the largest volume of fruit before summer heat; the fall crop frequently produces the best-quality fruit as temperatures cool. An excellent choice for Gulf South gardeners seeking a reliable, productive, disease-resistant red tomato with better heat tolerance than standard commercial varieties.

common_name: Better Bush Tomato (Compact Semi-Determinate Container Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Better Bush is outstanding in containers (at least 15-gallon) — its compact habit and sturdy self-supporting stems are ideally suited to patio, deck, and balcony growing.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Better Bush is one of the finest compact tomatoes for container gardening and small-space production — a semi-determinate hybrid that naturally stays 3–4 feet tall with sturdy, stocky stems that are largely self-supporting (minimal or no staking required), compact enough for large containers, and productive enough to provide a meaningful harvest without the sprawling, aggressive growth of indeterminate types. The medium-sized (8–10 oz), round, globe-shaped, bright-red fruits have good flavor and firm texture appropriate for fresh eating and all standard culinary uses. Better Bush carries disease resistance to V, F, and N — a standard Gulf South disease package. The compact size, self-supporting habit, and container suitability make Better Bush the first recommendation for zone 9 gardeners growing tomatoes on patios, balconies, or in raised beds where space is limited. For best container production, use containers of at least 15 gallons with premium potting mix and consistent watering and fertilization — container-grown tomatoes require more frequent irrigation and feeding than in-ground plants. Two plants per patio can provide significant summer tomato production with only 30–40 square feet of container space.

common_name: Floradade Tomato (Heat-Adapted Open-Pollinated Gulf South Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Floradade is an open-pollinated variety — seeds can be saved from the best fruits to maintain a self-sufficient seed supply across seasons.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Floradade is a University of Florida open-pollinated tomato variety developed specifically for Florida and Gulf South production — one of the older heat-tolerant open-pollinated varieties that has served as a reliable standard for Gulf South home garden production since its introduction. As an open-pollinated variety with good heat tolerance, Floradade produces medium-large (6–8 oz), round, firm, deep-red fruits with excellent fresh-eating and processing quality on a determinate plant that requires minimal staking. The disease resistance package includes Fusarium wilt race 1 — the most common race affecting Gulf South gardens. The primary practical advantage of Floradade over hybrid varieties is the ability to save seeds: since Floradade is open-pollinated, seeds saved from the best fruits grow true to type, allowing Gulf South gardeners to maintain a self-sufficient seed supply without annual seed purchases. This seed-saving advantage is significant for Gulf South gardeners who find locally-adapted, open-pollinated varieties often outperform northern hybrids in their specific climate. In zone 9, Floradade performs well in both spring and fall planting windows. The combination of local adaptation, open-pollination, heat tolerance, and reliable production has kept Floradade in production and recommendation by Gulf South cooperative extensions for decades.

common_name: Yellow Rain Lily (Bright Yellow Fall Zephyranthes Bulb)

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Yellow Rain Lily naturalizes freely in zone 8–10 gardens, self-seeding and producing offsets to form expanding colonies that require minimal maintenance — plant and allow to spread.

directions: Plant bulbs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Zephyranthes citrina (Yellow Rain Lily) is one of the finest low-growing, naturalizing bulbs for Gulf South gardens — producing small (2–3 inch), bright golden-yellow, funnel-shaped flowers on 6–8 inch stems in flushes that follow rainfall events from summer through fall. The characteristic blooming response to rainfall gives rain lilies their common name: a period of dry weather followed by rain triggers an almost simultaneous flowering display across an established colony — hundreds of flowers opening within days of a rain event, creating a carpet-effect display that is one of the most charming garden phenomena. Yellow Rain Lily is distinguished from the more common pink and white species by its pure, vivid golden-yellow color — a bright, clean yellow with no orange or orange tones. The small flowers are jewel-like individually and spectacular en masse. Zephyranthes citrina is among the most heat-adapted and drought-tolerant of the rain lilies — naturally adapted to Mexican and Central American seasonally dry habitats and outstanding in the heat and periodic drought of the Gulf South. Established bulbs multiply rapidly by offsets and self-seeding to form large, low-maintenance colonies that naturalize freely in turf edges, rock gardens, and border edges. Plant in masses of 25–50 or more for best display effect — individual bulbs disappear among other plantings.
